title: ksformat R Package — Claude Guide
text: ksformat provides SAS PROC FORMAT-like functionality R: - Value--label mappings (ks_format) label--value mappings (ks_invalue) - Proper handling NA, NULL, NaN missing values - Range-based formatting numeric data - SAS-style datetime formatting - Global format library reusable format definitions

title: ksformat R Package — Claude Guide
text: passing named vectors fnew(), direction depends type: - Character/numeric (default): c(Label = "Code") reversed → Code → Label - Value types (Date, POSIXct, logical): c(Key = value) used -data-driven formats, use fmap(keys, values) suppress auto-reversal types. ensures keys → values direction regardless type: Hand-written formats continue use R convention:
code: # Same pattern for character and Date formats fnew(fmap(ids, date_strings), type = "character") fnew(fmap(ids, dates), type = "Date") fnew(c(Male = "M", Female = "F"))  # auto-reversed: "M" → "Male" fnew("M" = "Male", "F" = "Female") # explicit: same result

title: ksformat R Package — Claude Guide

title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: ksformat usually described SAS PROC FORMAT-like tool ordinary value--label lookup. practice, package useful places clinical trial workflow needs plain dictionary: protocol-defined visit windows subject- arm-specific bucketing rules composite keys built multiple columns reverse lookups data cleaning QC report-ready numeric display listings summaries article shows non-standard uses come clinical trials adjacent data tasks.

title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: common clinical-trials task mapping visit-day values protocol-defined windows. idea extends arm-specific rules, one stratum slightly different window structure another. useful : visit window derivation ADaM outputs protocol deviation checks windowed endpoint calculations
code: windows <- fmap_strata(   stratum = c("ARM_A", "ARM_A", "ARM_A", "ARM_B", "ARM_B"),   low     = c(0,        7,       28,      0,       14),   high    = c(7,        28,      Inf,     14,      Inf),   label   = c("Baseline", "Wk1-3", "Wk4+", "Baseline", "Wk2+"),   inc_high = c(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE) )  fnew(   windows,   type = "stratified_range",   name = "visit_window",   .other = "Outside window" )  visit_df <- tibble(   arm = c("ARM_A", "ARM_A", "ARM_B", "ARM_B", "ARM_C"),   day = c(3, 35, 5, 40, 10) ) |> mutate(   window = fputk(arm, day, format = "visit_window"), )  visit_df

title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: Clinical trial data often uses multi-column logic: lab category + test code + unit, population + analysis flag + visit type. fputk() can build lookup multiple fields without manually concatenating analysis step. pattern useful : AE grouping term + severity + relation CM coding medication + route + dose form endpoint derivations multiple source fields
code: fnew(   fmap(     paste(       c("BLOOD CHEMISTRY", "COAGULOGRAM", "COAGULATION PANEL", "COAGULOGRAM"),       c("BLOOD",           "BLOOD",        "BLOOD",             "BLOOD"),       c("ALB",             "FIBRINO",      "INR",               "INR"),       c("g/L",             "g/L",           NA,                  NA),       sep = "|"     ),     c("ALB", "FIBRINO", "INR", "INR")   ),   type = "character",   name = "lb_param",   ignore_case = TRUE,   .other = NA )  lab_df <- tibble(   LBCAT = c("BLOOD CHEMISTRY", "COAGULOGRAM", "COAGULATION PANEL"),   LBSPEC = c("BLOOD", "BLOOD", "BLOOD"),   LBTESTCD = c("ALB", "INR", "INR"),   LBSTRESU = c("g/L", NA, NA) ) |> mutate(   PARAMCD = fputk(LBCAT, LBSPEC, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESU, format = "lb_param", na_as_string = TRUE) )  lab_df

what: 3. Reverse lookup for QC and cleaning
title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: finputk() helpful raw labels must normalized back codes. clinical programming, makes suitable QC checks, mapping vendor text, validating reviewer-facing labels. Typical uses include: deriving internal codes decoded labels QC datasets validating data entry controlled terminology reconciling spreadsheet exports back source codes
code: finput(   fmap(     paste(c("PLACEBO", "DRUG A", "DRUG B"), c("PBO", "A50", "B100"), sep = "|"),     c(1L, 2L, 3L)   ),   target_type = "integer",   name = "trt_code_inv" )  trt_df <- tibble(   ARMN = c("PLACEBO", "DRUG A", "DRUG B", "UNKNOWN"),   TRTCD = c("PBO", "A50", "B100", "X") ) |> mutate(   TRTSEQ = finputk(ARMN, TRTCD, invalue_name = "trt_code_inv") )  trt_df

title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: ksformat can also used report output needs consistent numeric presentation. lookup table; pattern-driven formatter. result achieved using expression labels, base R, sprintf() support thousands separators commas. can use formatC formatting purposes. Pattern-driven formatter looks simplier case. useful : CSR TLF output generation QA listings counts rates ad hoc review tables consistent display matters
code: fnew("$%,.2f", type = "numeric", name = "currency") fnew("%.1f%%", type = "numeric", name = "pct")  summary_df <- tibble(   metric = c("Cost", "Response rate"),   raw = c(1234.56, 0.153) ) |> mutate(   display = c(fputn(raw[1], "currency"), fputn(raw[2] * 100, "pct")) )  summary_df stat_fmt <- fnew(   #"currency"   = "sprintf('$%,.2f', .x1)", # Will not work!   "currency"   = "formatC(.x1, digits = 2, big.mark = ',', format = 'f', decimal.mark = '.')",   "pct" = "sprintf('%.1f%%', .x1 * 100)",   name = "stat",   type = "character" )  summary_df <- tibble(   metric = c("Cost", "Response rate"),   raw = c(1234.56, 0.153),   format = c("currency","pct") ) |> mutate(   display = fput(format, stat_fmt, raw) )  summary_df

title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: Clinical teams often work periods screening, treatment, follow-, analysis cut-offs. date_range datetime_range let keep rules one place instead duplicating ad hoc code.
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE study_period (date_range)   [2024-01-01, 2024-02-01) = "Screening"   [2024-02-01, 2024-06-01) = "Treatment"   [2024-06-01, HIGH]       = "Follow-up"   .missing                 = "No date" ; ')  obs_dates <- as.Date(c("2024-01-15", "2024-03-10", "2024-07-01", NA)) tibble(   date = obs_dates,   period = fput(obs_dates, "study_period") )

what: 8. fparse() syntax for all format families
title: Non-standard Applications of ksformat for Clinical Trials and Other Tasks
text: want express fnew()-style rules text, fparse() supports VALUE/INVALUE families package parser. main idea : use VALUE forward lookup formats use INVALUE reverse lookup formats use pattern = ... date/time/datetime formats use pattern: ... VALUE header numeric display patterns use interval syntax numeric, date, datetime, stratified ranges direct fparse() equivalent fnew_date(). blocks, pattern = ... right way specify display format. Numeric display formatting now parser syntax. Put pattern VALUE header keep block otherwise empty except optional .missing / .directives. Range formats expressed interval notation. syntax works numeric, date_range, datetime_range blocks. stratified_range combines stratum inner range. parser accepts friendly STRATUM|[low, high) form. fnew() remains concise way create report-formatting object already know want display pattern. fparse() useful rule needs stored text, versioned spec, generated another tool.
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE sex (character)   "M" = "Male"   "F" = "Female"   .missing = "Unknown"   .other = "Other" ;  VALUE age_group (numeric)   [0, 18)    = "Child"   [18, 65)   = "Adult"   [65, HIGH] = "Senior" ; ') fparse(text = ' INVALUE sex_inv   "Male" = "M"   "Female" = "F"   .missing = "U" ; ') fparse(text = ' VALUE enrldt (date)   pattern = "DATE9."   .missing = "Not Enrolled" ;  VALUE visit_time (time)   pattern = "TIME8." ;  VALUE stamp (datetime)   pattern = "DATETIME20." ; ') fparse(text = ' VALUE currency (numeric, pattern: "$%,.2f")   .missing = "NO DATA" ;  VALUE pct (numeric, pattern: "%.1f%%") ; ')  tibble(   raw = c(1234.56, -7890.12, 0, NA),   currency = fputn(c(1234.56, -7890.12, 0, NA), "currency"),   pct = fputn(c(0, 12.3, -4.5, NA), "pct") ) fparse(text = ' VALUE study_period (date_range)   [2024-01-01, 2024-02-01) = "Screening"   [2024-02-01, 2024-06-01) = "Treatment"   [2024-06-01, HIGH]       = "Follow-up" ;  VALUE shift (datetime_range)   [2024-01-15 00:00, 2024-01-15 08:00) = "Night"   [2024-01-15 08:00, 2024-01-15 16:00) = "Day"   [2024-01-15 16:00, 2024-01-16 00:00) = "Evening" ; ') fparse(text = ' VALUE visit_window (stratified_range, range_subtype: numeric)   "ARM_A"|[0, 7)     = "Baseline"   "ARM_A"|[7, 28)    = "Wk1-3"   "ARM_B"|[0, 14)    = "Baseline"   .other              = "Outside" ; ') fnew("$%,.2f", type = "numeric", name = "currency_direct") fputn(c(1234.56, -7890.12, 0), "currency_direct")

text: ksformat limited simple code--label dictionaries. clinical trials can also serve compact rule engine visit windows, composite keys, reverse mappings, report formatting, small narrative fragments. makes useful anywhere repeated conditional logic otherwise spread across scripts, spreadsheets, one-helper functions.
code: fclear()

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Create format gender codes (auto-stored library “sex”):
code: fnew(   "M" = "Male",   "F" = "Female",   .missing = "Unknown",   .other = "Other Gender",   name = "sex" )  gender_codes <- c("M", "F", "M", NA, "X", "F") formatted_genders <- fput(gender_codes, "sex")  data.frame(   code = gender_codes,   label = formatted_genders ) #>   code        label #> 1    M         Male #> 2    F       Female #> 3    M         Male #> 4 <NA>      Unknown #> 5    X Other Gender #> 6    F       Female  fprint("sex") #> KS Format:sex #> Type: character  #> Mappings: #>   M => Male #>   F => Female #>   .missing => Unknown #>   .other => Other Gender

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Define formats SAS-like text (auto-registered):
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE age (numeric)   [0, 18)     = "Child"   [18, 65)    = "Adult"   [65, HIGH]  = "Senior"   .missing    = "Age Unknown" ; ')  ages <- c(5, 15.3, 17.9, 18, 45, 64.99, 65, 85, NA) age_groups <- fputn(ages, "age")  data.frame(   age = ages,   group = age_groups ) #>     age       group #> 1  5.00       Child #> 2 15.30       Child #> 3 17.90       Child #> 4 18.00       Adult #> 5 45.00       Adult #> 6 64.99       Adult #> 7 65.00      Senior #> 8 85.00      Senior #> 9    NA Age Unknown

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE bmi (numeric)   [0, 18.5)    = "Underweight"   [18.5, 25)   = "Normal"   [25, 30)     = "Overweight"   [30, HIGH]   = "Obese"   .missing     = "No data" ; ')  bmi_values <- c(16.2, 18.5, 22.7, 25, 29.9, 35.1, NA) bmi_labels <- fputn(bmi_values, "bmi")  data.frame(   bmi = bmi_values,   category = bmi_labels ) #>    bmi    category #> 1 16.2 Underweight #> 2 18.5      Normal #> 3 22.7      Normal #> 4 25.0  Overweight #> 5 29.9  Overweight #> 6 35.1       Obese #> 7   NA     No data

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE score (numeric)   (0, 50]    = "Low"   (50, 100]  = "High"   .other     = "Out of range" ; ')  scores <- c(0, 1, 50, 51, 100, 101) score_labels <- fputn(scores, "score")  data.frame(   score = scores,   label = score_labels ) #>   score        label #> 1     0 Out of range #> 2     1          Low #> 3    50          Low #> 4    51         High #> 5   100         High #> 6   101 Out of range

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Invalues convert labels back values. default target_type "numeric":
code: finput(   "Male" = 1,   "Female" = 2,   name = "sex_inv" ) #> KS Invalue: sex_inv #> Target Type: numeric  #> Mappings: #>   Male => 1 #>   Female => 2  labels <- c("Male", "Female", "Male", "Unknown", "Female") codes <- finputn(labels, "sex_inv")  data.frame(   label = labels,   code = codes ) #>     label code #> 1    Male    1 #> 2  Female    2 #> 3    Male    1 #> 4 Unknown   NA #> 5  Female    2

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: fnew_bid() creates format invalue :
code: status_bi <- fnew_bid(   "A" = "Active",   "I" = "Inactive",   "P" = "Pending",   name = "status" )  # Forward: code -> label status_codes <- c("A", "I", "P", "A") status_labels <- fputc(status_codes, "status") data.frame(code = status_codes, label = status_labels) #>   code    label #> 1    A   Active #> 2    I Inactive #> 3    P  Pending #> 4    A   Active  # Reverse: label -> code test_labels <- c("Active", "Pending", "Inactive") test_codes <- finputc(test_labels, "status_inv") data.frame(label = test_labels, code = test_codes) #>      label code #> 1   Active    A #> 2  Pending    P #> 3 Inactive    I

what: Example 7: Parse Multiple Formats from Text
title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fparse(text = ' // Study format definitions  VALUE race (character)   "W" = "White"   "B" = "Black"   "A" = "Asian"   .missing = "Unknown" ;  INVALUE race_inv   "White" = 1   "Black" = 2   "Asian" = 3 ; ')  flist()   # character vector of names #> [1] "age"        "bmi"        "race"       "race_inv"   "score"      #> [6] "sex"        "sex_inv"    "status"     "status_inv" fprint() #> Registered formats: #>   age - VALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   bmi - VALUE (numeric), 4 mapping(s) #>   race - VALUE (character), 3 mapping(s) #>   race_inv - INVALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   score - VALUE (numeric), 2 mapping(s) #>   sex - VALUE (character), 2 mapping(s) #>   sex_inv - INVALUE (numeric), 2 mapping(s) #>   status - VALUE (character), 3 mapping(s) #>   status_inv - INVALUE (character), 3 mapping(s)

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: df <- data.frame(   id = 1:6,   sex = c("M", "F", "M", "F", NA, "X"),   age = c(15, 25, 45, 70, 35, NA),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  sex_f <- format_get("sex") age_f <- format_get("age")  df_formatted <- fput_df(   df,   sex = sex_f,   age = age_f,   suffix = "_label" )  df_formatted #>   id  sex age    sex_label   age_label #> 1  1    M  15         Male       Child #> 2  2    F  25       Female       Adult #> 3  3    M  45         Male       Adult #> 4  4    F  70       Female      Senior #> 5  5 <NA>  35      Unknown       Adult #> 6  6    X  NA Other Gender Age Unknown

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: SAS date format names auto-resolved — pre-creation needed:
code: today <- Sys.Date()  data.frame(   format = c("DATE9.", "MMDDYY10.", "DDMMYY10.", "YYMMDD10.",              "MONYY7.", "WORDDATE.", "YEAR4.", "QTR."),   result = c(     fputn(today, "DATE9."),     fputn(today, "MMDDYY10."),     fputn(today, "DDMMYY10."),     fputn(today, "YYMMDD10."),     fputn(today, "MONYY7."),     fputn(today, "WORDDATE."),     fputn(today, "YEAR4."),     fputn(today, "QTR.")   ) ) #>      format        result #> 1    DATE9.     09JUL2026 #> 2 MMDDYY10.    07/09/2026 #> 3 DDMMYY10.    09/07/2026 #> 4 YYMMDD10.    2026-07-09 #> 5   MONYY7.       JUL2026 #> 6 WORDDATE. July 09, 2026 #> 7    YEAR4.          2026 #> 8      QTR.             3  # Multiple dates dates <- as.Date(c("2020-01-15", "2020-06-30", "2020-12-25")) fputn(dates, "DATE9.") #> [1] "15JAN2020" "30JUN2020" "25DEC2020"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: now <- Sys.time()  data.frame(   format = c("DATETIME20.", "DATETIME13.", "DTDATE.", "DTYYMMDD."),   result = c(     fputn(now, "DATETIME20."),     fputn(now, "DATETIME13."),     fputn(now, "DTDATE."),     fputn(now, "DTYYMMDD.")   ) ) #>        format             result #> 1 DATETIME20. 09JUL2026:14:51:39 #> 2 DATETIME13.      09JUL26:14:51 #> 3     DTDATE.          09JUL2026 #> 4   DTYYMMDD.         2026-07-09  # From numeric R-epoch seconds r_secs <- as.numeric(as.POSIXct("2025-06-15 14:30:00", tz = "UTC")) fputn(r_secs, "DATETIME20.") #> [1] "15JUN2025:14:30:00"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: # SAS-named format fnew_date("DATE9.", name = "bday_fmt") #> KS Format:bday_fmt #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %d%b%Y (DATE9.) birthdays <- as.Date(c("1990-03-25", "1985-11-03", "2000-07-14")) fput(birthdays, "bday_fmt") #> [1] "25MAR1990" "03NOV1985" "14JUL2000"  # Custom strftime pattern (e.g. DD.MM.YYYY) fnew_date("%d.%m.%Y", name = "ru_date", type = "date") #> KS Format:ru_date #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %d.%m.%Y fput(birthdays, "ru_date") #> [1] "25.03.1990" "03.11.1985" "14.07.2000"  # Custom pattern with missing label fnew_date("MMDDYY10.", name = "us_date", .missing = "NO DATE") #> KS Format:us_date #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %m/%d/%Y (MMDDYY10.)  #>   .missing => NO DATE mixed <- c(as.Date("2025-01-01"), NA, as.Date("2025-12-31")) fput(mixed, "us_date") #> [1] "01/01/2025" "NO DATE"    "12/31/2025"  fprint("bday_fmt") #> KS Format:bday_fmt #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %d%b%Y (DATE9.)

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: patients <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   visit_date = as.Date(c("2025-01-10", "2025-02-15", "2025-03-20", NA)),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  visit_fmt <- fnew_date("DATE9.", name = "visit_fmt", .missing = "NOT RECORDED") fput_df(patients, visit_date = visit_fmt) #>   id visit_date visit_date_fmt #> 1  1 2025-01-10      10JAN2025 #> 2  2 2025-02-15      15FEB2025 #> 3  3 2025-03-20      20MAR2025 #> 4  4       <NA>   NOT RECORDED

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE enrldt (date)   pattern = "DATE9."   .missing = "Not Enrolled" ;  VALUE visit_time (time)   pattern = "TIME8." ;  VALUE stamp (datetime)   pattern = "DATETIME20." ; ')  fput(as.Date("2025-03-01"), "enrldt") #> [1] "01MAR2025" fput(36000, "visit_time") #> [1] "10:00:00" fput(as.POSIXct("2025-03-01 10:00:00", tz = "UTC"), "stamp") #> [1] "01MAR2025:10:00:00"  # Export back to text enrl_obj <- format_get("enrldt") cat(fexport(enrldt = enrl_obj)) #> VALUE enrldt (date) #>   pattern = "DATE9." #>   .missing = "Not Enrolled" #> ;  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: multilabel formats, single value can match multiple labels:
code: fnew(   "0,5,TRUE,TRUE"    = "Infant",   "6,11,TRUE,TRUE"   = "Child",   "12,17,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Adolescent",   "0,17,TRUE,TRUE"   = "Pediatric",   "18,64,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Adult",   "65,Inf,TRUE,TRUE" = "Elderly",   "18,Inf,TRUE,TRUE" = "Non-Pediatric",   name = "age_categories",   type = "numeric",   multilabel = TRUE )  ages <- c(3, 14, 25, 70)  # fput returns first match only fput(ages, "age_categories") #> [1] "Infant"        "Pediatric"     "Adult"         "Non-Pediatric"  # fput_all returns ALL matching labels all_labels <- fput_all(ages, "age_categories") for (i in seq_along(ages)) {   cat("Age", ages[i], "->", paste(all_labels[[i]], collapse = ", "), "\n") } #> Age 3 -> Infant, Pediatric  #> Age 14 -> Pediatric, Adolescent  #> Age 25 -> Adult, Non-Pediatric  #> Age 70 -> Non-Pediatric, Elderly

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fnew(   "0,100,TRUE,TRUE"   = "Valid Score",   "0,49,TRUE,TRUE"    = "Below Average",   "50,100,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Above Average",   "90,100,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Excellent",   .missing = "No Score",   .other = "Out of Range",   name = "score_ml",   type = "numeric",   multilabel = TRUE )  scores <- c(95, 45, NA, 150) ml_result <- fput_all(scores, "score_ml")  for (i in seq_along(scores)) {   cat("Score", ifelse(is.na(scores[i]), "NA", scores[i]),       "->", paste(ml_result[[i]], collapse = ", "), "\n") } #> Score 95 -> Valid Score, Above Average, Excellent  #> Score 45 -> Below Average, Valid Score  #> Score NA -> No Score  #> Score 150 -> Out of Range

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE risk (numeric, multilabel)   [0, 3]   = "Low Risk"   [0, 7]   = "Monitored"   (3, 7]   = "Medium Risk"   (7, 10]  = "High Risk" ; ')  risk_scores <- c(2, 5, 9) risk_labels <- fput_all(risk_scores, "risk") for (i in seq_along(risk_scores)) {   cat("Score", risk_scores[i], "->",       paste(risk_labels[[i]], collapse = " | "), "\n") } #> Score 2 -> Low Risk | Monitored  #> Score 5 -> Monitored | Medium Risk  #> Score 9 -> High Risk

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: risk_obj <- format_get("risk") cat(fexport(risk = risk_obj)) #> VALUE risk (numeric, multilabel) #>   [0, 3] = "Low Risk" #>   [0, 7] = "Monitored" #>   (3, 7] = "Medium Risk" #>   (7, 10] = "High Risk" #> ;  fprint("risk") #> KS Format:risk (multilabel) #> Type: numeric  #> Mappings: #>   [0, 3] => Low Risk #>   [0, 7] => Monitored #>   (3, 7] => Medium Risk #>   (7, 10] => High Risk

what: Practical Example: Adverse Event Severity Grading
title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fnew(   "1,1,TRUE,TRUE" = "Mild",   "2,2,TRUE,TRUE" = "Moderate",   "3,3,TRUE,TRUE" = "Severe",   "4,4,TRUE,TRUE" = "Life-threatening",   "5,5,TRUE,TRUE" = "Fatal",   "3,5,TRUE,TRUE" = "Serious",   "1,2,TRUE,TRUE" = "Non-serious",   name = "ae_grade",   type = "numeric",   multilabel = TRUE )  grades <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) ae_labels <- fput_all(grades, "ae_grade") for (i in seq_along(grades)) {   cat("Grade", grades[i], ":",       paste(ae_labels[[i]], collapse = " + "), "\n") } #> Grade 1 : Mild + Non-serious  #> Grade 2 : Non-serious + Moderate  #> Grade 3 : Severe + Serious  #> Grade 4 : Serious + Life-threatening  #> Grade 5 : Serious + Fatal  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: sex_nc <- fnew(   "M" = "Male",   "F" = "Female",   .missing = "Unknown",   name = "sex_nc",   type = "character",   ignore_case = TRUE )  input <- c("m", "F", "M", "f", NA) fput(input, sex_nc) #> [1] "Male"    "Female"  "Male"    "Female"  "Unknown"  # Note the [nocase] flag fprint("sex_nc") #> KS Format:sex_nc (nocase) #> Type: character  #> Mappings: #>   M => Male #>   F => Female #>   .missing => Unknown  # Also works with fputc fputc("m", "sex_nc") #> [1] "Male"  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Expression labels contain .x1, .x2, etc., reference extra arguments passed fput(). lets compute labels dynamically.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: stat_fmt <- fnew(   "n"   = "sprintf('%s', .x1)",   "pct" = "sprintf('%.1f%%', .x1 * 100)",   name = "stat",   type = "character" )  types  <- c("n",  "pct",  "n",   "pct") values <- c(42,   0.053,  100,   0.255)  fput(types, stat_fmt, values) #> [1] "42"    "5.3%"  "100"   "25.5%"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: ratio_fmt <- fnew(   "ratio" = "sprintf('%s/%s', .x1, .x2)",   name = "ratio",   type = "character" )  fput("ratio", ratio_fmt, 3, 10) #> [1] "3/10" fput(c("ratio", "ratio"), ratio_fmt, c(3, 7), c(10, 20)) #> [1] "3/10" "7/20"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: sign_fmt <- fnew(   "val" = "ifelse(.x1 > 0, paste0('+', .x1), as.character(.x1))",   name = "sign",   type = "character" )  nums <- c(5, 0, -3) fput(rep("val", 3), sign_fmt, nums) #> [1] "+5" "0"  "-3"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: mixed_fmt <- fnew(   "header" = "HEADER",   "n"      = "sprintf('N=%s', .x1)",   "pct"    = "sprintf('%.1f%%', .x1 * 100)",   name = "mixed",   type = "character" )  keys <- c("header", "n", "pct", "header", "n") vals <- c(0,        42,  0.15,  0,        100) fput(keys, mixed_fmt, vals) #> [1] "HEADER" "N=42"   "15.0%"  "HEADER" "N=100"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: known_fmt <- fnew(   "ok" = "OK",   .other = "sprintf('Error(%s)', .x1)",   name = "err_fmt",   type = "character" )  codes   <- c("ok", "E01", "ok", "E99") details <- c("",   "timeout", "", "overflow") fput(codes, known_fmt, details) #> [1] "OK"              "Error(timeout)"  "OK"              "Error(overflow)"

title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: label_fmt <- fnew(   "val" = "sprintf('%s (N=%s)', .x1, .x2)",   name = "recycle",   type = "character" )  fput(c("val", "val"), label_fmt, c(42, 55), 100) #> [1] "42 (N=100)" "55 (N=100)"

what: Statistical Table Format with Computed Labels
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: realistic clinical-trial example: e() marks labels expressions evaluated apply-time, .x1 references extra argument, multiline dplyr::case_when shows complex conditional formatting. format can created via fparse(). Note multiline expressions must collapsed single lines text block, (eval) marks evaluated labels: stat (via fnew) stat_01 (via fparse) produce identical results:
code: # Population counts used as denominators n.trt <- data.frame(pop = c("fas","pps","saf"), ntot = c(34, 30, 36)) get_n <- function(pop) {   n.trt$ntot[n.trt$pop == pop] }  fnew(   "n_fas" = e("get_n('fas')"),   "n_pps" = e("get_n('pps')"),   "n_saf" = e("get_n('saf')"),   "n"   = "sprintf('%d', .x1)",   "n_pct_fas" = "sprintf('%d (%5.1f%%)', .x1, .x1 * 100 / get_n('fas'))",   "n_pct_pps" = "sprintf('%d (%5.1f%%)', .x1, .x1 * 100 / get_n('pps'))",   "n_pct_saf" = "sprintf('%d (%5.1f%%)', .x1, .x1 * 100 / get_n('saf'))",   "pct" = "dplyr::case_when(                .x1>0 & .x1<0.1 ~ sprintf('%5s', ' <0.1%'),                .x1>=0.1 | .x1==0 ~ sprintf(paste0('%5.', 1 ,'f%%'), .x1)            )",   "pval" = "dplyr::case_when(                 .x1>=0 & .x1<0.001 ~ sprintf('%s', '<0.001'),                 .x1>=0.001 & .x1<=0.999 ~ sprintf(paste0('%.', 3 ,'f'), .x1),                 .x1>0.999 ~ sprintf('%s', '>0.999'), .default = '--'            )",   name = "stat",   type = "character" ) fmt <- '   VALUE stat_01 (character)      "n_fas" = "get_n(\'fas\')" (eval)      "n_pps" = "get_n(\'pps\')" (eval)      "n_saf" = "get_n(\'saf\')" (eval)      "n"     = "sprintf(\'%d\', .x1)"      "pct"   = "dplyr::case_when(.x1>0 & .x1<0.1 ~ sprintf(\'%5s\', \' <0.1%\'), .x1>=0.1 | .x1==0 ~ sprintf(paste0(\'%5.\', 1 ,\'f%%\'), .x1))"      "n_pct_fas" = "sprintf(\'%d (%5.1f%%)\', .x1, .x1 * 100 / get_n(\'fas\'))"      "n_pct_pps" = "sprintf(\'%d (%5.1f%%)\', .x1, .x1 * 100 / get_n(\'pps\'))"      "n_pct_saf" = "sprintf(\'%d (%5.1f%%)\', .x1, .x1 * 100 / get_n(\'saf\'))"      "pval"  = "dplyr::case_when(.x1>=0 & .x1<0.001 ~ sprintf(\'%s\', \'<0.001\'), .x1>=0.001 & .x1<=0.999 ~ sprintf(paste0(\'%.\', 3 ,\'f\'), .x1), .x1>0.999 ~ sprintf(\'%s\', \'>0.999\'), .default = \'--\')" ;' fparse(fmt) df <- data.frame(   types = c("n_fas", "n_pps", "n_saf", "n", "pct", "pct", "n", "pval", "pval",             "n_pct_fas", "n_pct_pps", "n_pct_saf"),   values = c(NA, NA, NA, 42, 0.053, 0.0008, 100, 0.255, 0.0003, 22, 22, 22) )  df$fmt    <- fput(df$types, "stat",    df$values) df$fmt_01 <- fput(df$types, "stat_01", df$values) print(df) #>        types   values         fmt      fmt_01 #> 1      n_fas       NA          34          34 #> 2      n_pps       NA          30          30 #> 3      n_saf       NA          36          36 #> 4          n  42.0000          42          42 #> 5        pct   0.0530       <0.1%       <0.1% #> 6        pct   0.0008       <0.1%       <0.1% #> 7          n 100.0000         100         100 #> 8       pval   0.2550       0.255       0.255 #> 9       pval   0.0003      <0.001      <0.001 #> 10 n_pct_fas  22.0000 22 ( 64.7%) 22 ( 64.7%) #> 11 n_pct_pps  22.0000 22 ( 73.3%) 22 ( 73.3%) #> 12 n_pct_saf  22.0000 22 ( 61.1%) 22 ( 61.1%)  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: element can use different format, determined vector format names:
code: # Dispatch format: maps type code to format name fnew("1" = "groupx", "2" = "groupy", "3" = "groupz",      name = "typefmt", type = "numeric")  # Per-group character formats fnew("positive" = "agree",  "negative" = "disagree", "neutral" = "notsure",      name = "groupx", type = "character") fnew("positive" = "accept", "negative" = "reject",   "neutral" = "possible",      name = "groupy", type = "character") fnew("positive" = "pass",   "negative" = "fail",     "neutral" = "retest",      name = "groupz", type = "character")  type     <- c(1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3) response <- c("positive", "negative", "neutral",               "positive", "negative", "neutral",               "positive", "negative", "neutral")  # Step 1: map type -> format name respfmt <- fput(type, "typefmt")  # Step 2: apply per-element format word <- fputc(response, respfmt)  data.frame(type = type, response = response, respfmt = respfmt, word = word) #>   type response respfmt     word #> 1    1 positive  groupx    agree #> 2    1 negative  groupx disagree #> 3    1  neutral  groupx  notsure #> 4    2 positive  groupy   accept #> 5    2 negative  groupy   reject #> 6    2  neutral  groupy possible #> 7    3 positive  groupz     pass #> 8    3 negative  groupz     fail #> 9    3  neutral  groupz   retest  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 17: Working with Dates and Formats — PUTN
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: SAS-style workflow format names looked dynamically per observation:
code: # Format that maps key codes to date format names fnew("1" = "date9.", "2" = "mmddyy10.",      name = "writfmt", type = "numeric")  fnew_date("date9.") #> KS Format:DATE9. #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %d%b%Y (DATE9.) fnew_date("mmddyy10.") #> KS Format:MMDDYY10. #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %m/%d/%Y (MMDDYY10.)  # Input data (R date numbers = days since 1970-01-01) number <- c(12103, 10899) key    <- c(1, 2)  # Look up format name per observation datefmt <- fputn(key, "writfmt")  # Apply per-element date format date <- fputn(number, datefmt)  data.frame(number = number, key = key, datefmt = datefmt, date = date) #>   number key   datefmt       date #> 1  12103   1    date9.  20FEB2003 #> 2  10899   2 mmddyy10. 11/04/1999  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 18: Import SAS Formats from CNTLOUT CSV
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: fimport() function reads CSV file exported SAS format catalogue (PROC FORMAT ... CNTLOUT=):
code: csv_path <- system.file("extdata", "test_cntlout.csv", package = "ksformat") imported <- fimport(csv_path) #> Warning: Skipping PICTURE format: "PICFMT" #> ℹ TYPE="P" is not supported by ksformat. #> Warning: Skipped incompatible entry in format "SMISSING": #> ✖ SAS special missing value '.A' (HLO='S') has no R equivalent. #> Warning: Skipped incompatible entry in format "SMISSING": #> ✖ SAS special missing value '.B' (HLO='S') has no R equivalent. #> ✔ Imported 4 formats and 1 invalue from #>   /private/var/folders/rn/3s0h46m118j426j_fmjr1z8m0000gn/T/RtmpLTJGZ4/temp_libpathf0d97beed76a/ksformat/extdata/test_cntlout.csv. names(imported) #> [1] "AGEGRP"   "BMICAT"   "GENDER"   "RACEIN"   "SMISSING"  flist() #> [1] "AGEGRP"   "BMICAT"   "GENDER"   "RACEIN"   "SMISSING" fprint() #> Registered formats: #>   AGEGRP - VALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   BMICAT - VALUE (numeric), 4 mapping(s) #>   GENDER - VALUE (character), 2 mapping(s) #>   RACEIN - INVALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   SMISSING - VALUE (numeric), 1 mapping(s)

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Expression labels can select languages apply-time using extra argument:
code: # Single format, language selected via .x1 extra argument sex_bi <- fnew(   "M" = "ifelse(.x1 == 'en', 'Male', 'Homme')",   "F" = "ifelse(.x1 == 'en', 'Female', 'Femme')",   .missing = "Unknown",   name = "sex_bi" )  # .x1 = language code per observation fput(c("M", "F", "M"), sex_bi, c("en", "fr", "en")) #> [1] "Male"  "Femme" "Male" # -> "Male" "Femme" "Male"  # Alternative: one format per language, selected at apply-time fnew("M" = "Male",  "F" = "Female",  .missing = "Unknown", name = "sex_en") fnew("M" = "Homme", "F" = "Femme",   .missing = "Inconnu", name = "sex_fr")  lang <- "fr" fput(c("M", "F", NA), paste0("sex_", lang)) #> [1] "Homme"   "Femme"   "Inconnu" # -> "Homme" "Femme" "Inconnu"  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 20: Composite Key Lookup with fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: fputk() pastes multiple vectors composite key format lookup. useful format keyed combination several columns, common pattern clinical data (e.g., looking visit date subject + visit number).
code: # Simulate a Subject Visits (SV) domain SV <- data.frame(   USUBJID  = c("SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-002", "SUBJ-002"),   VISITNUM = c(1, 2, 3, 1, 2),   SVSTDTC  = c("2025-01-15", "2025-02-20", "2025-03-10",                "2025-01-18", "2025-02-25"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Simulate a Questionnaires (QS) domain QS <- data.frame(   USUBJID  = c("SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-002", "SUBJ-002", "SUBJ-002"),   VISITNUM = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 3),   QSTESTCD = c("SCORE1", "SCORE1", "SCORE1", "SCORE1", "SCORE1"),   QSSTRESN = c(85, 90, 72, 78, NA),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  SV #>    USUBJID VISITNUM    SVSTDTC #> 1 SUBJ-001        1 2025-01-15 #> 2 SUBJ-001        2 2025-02-20 #> 3 SUBJ-001        3 2025-03-10 #> 4 SUBJ-002        1 2025-01-18 #> 5 SUBJ-002        2 2025-02-25 QS #>    USUBJID VISITNUM QSTESTCD QSSTRESN #> 1 SUBJ-001        1   SCORE1       85 #> 2 SUBJ-001        2   SCORE1       90 #> 3 SUBJ-002        1   SCORE1       72 #> 4 SUBJ-002        2   SCORE1       78 #> 5 SUBJ-002        3   SCORE1       NA

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Register format keyed USUBJID|VISITNUM values visit start date (SVSTDTC) character strings: Now look visit dates QS domain using fputk():
code: # Create composite key -> date string mapping from SV fnew(   fmap(paste(SV$USUBJID, SV$VISITNUM, sep = "|"), SV$SVSTDTC),   .other  = "NOT FOUND",   name    = "svdtc",   type    = "character",   ignore_case = TRUE )  fprint("svdtc") #> KS Format:svdtc (nocase) #> Type: character  #> Mappings: #>   SUBJ-001|1 => 2025-01-15 #>   SUBJ-001|2 => 2025-02-20 #>   SUBJ-001|3 => 2025-03-10 #>   SUBJ-002|1 => 2025-01-18 #>   SUBJ-002|2 => 2025-02-25 #>   .other => NOT FOUND QS$SVSTDTC <- fputk(QS$USUBJID, QS$VISITNUM, format = "svdtc") QS #>    USUBJID VISITNUM QSTESTCD QSSTRESN    SVSTDTC #> 1 SUBJ-001        1   SCORE1       85 2025-01-15 #> 2 SUBJ-001        2   SCORE1       90 2025-02-20 #> 3 SUBJ-002        1   SCORE1       72 2025-01-18 #> 4 SUBJ-002        2   SCORE1       78 2025-02-25 #> 5 SUBJ-002        3   SCORE1       NA  NOT FOUND class(QS$SVSTDTC)  # character #> [1] "character"  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

previous_headings: Example 20: Composite Key Lookup with fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Using type = "Date", values stored native R Date objects fput()/fputk() return directly — string conversion needed:
code: # Create composite key -> Date mapping from SV fnew(   fmap(     paste(SV$USUBJID, SV$VISITNUM, sep = "|"),     as.Date(SV$SVSTDTC, format = "%Y-%m-%d")   ),   .other  = NA,   name    = "svdtn",   type    = "Date",   ignore_case = TRUE )  fprint("svdtn") #> KS Format:svdtn (nocase) #> Type: Date  #> Mappings: #>   SUBJ-001|1 => 2025-01-15 #>   SUBJ-001|2 => 2025-02-20 #>   SUBJ-001|3 => 2025-03-10 #>   SUBJ-002|1 => 2025-01-18 #>   SUBJ-002|2 => 2025-02-25 QS$SVSTDTC_DT <- fputk(QS$USUBJID, QS$VISITNUM, format = "svdtn") QS #>    USUBJID VISITNUM QSTESTCD QSSTRESN    SVSTDTC SVSTDTC_DT #> 1 SUBJ-001        1   SCORE1       85 2025-01-15 2025-01-15 #> 2 SUBJ-001        2   SCORE1       90 2025-02-20 2025-02-20 #> 3 SUBJ-002        1   SCORE1       72 2025-01-18 2025-01-18 #> 4 SUBJ-002        2   SCORE1       78 2025-02-25 2025-02-25 #> 5 SUBJ-002        3   SCORE1       NA  NOT FOUND       <NA> class(QS$SVSTDTC_DT)  # Date #> [1] "Date"  # Typed NA for unmatched keys (SUBJ-002 Visit 3 not in SV) is.na(QS$SVSTDTC_DT[5]) #> [1] TRUE  # Date arithmetic works directly QS$SVSTDTC_DT + 7  # add 7 days #> [1] "2025-01-22" "2025-02-27" "2025-01-25" "2025-03-04" NA  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 21: Consistent Data-Driven Formats with fmap()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: building formats data (e.g., data frame 1000+ rows), need named vector mapping keys values. default, fnew() treats named vectors differently depending output type: Value types (Date, POSIXct, logical): c(key = value) — natural direction, reversal. Character / numeric: c(Label = "Code") — R convention, names values swapped internally. inconsistency confusing data-driven formats. fmap() helper solves : fmap(keys, values) works identically types.

previous_headings: Example 21: Consistent Data-Driven Formats with fmap()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Suppose demographics dataset need two lookup formats data — one returning Date objects, one returning character strings:
code: library(ksformat)  dm <- data.frame(   USUBJID = c("SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-002", "SUBJ-003"),   SUBJID  = c("001", "002", "003"),   RFICDTC = c("2023-03-09T08:45", "2024-08-13T09:53", "2025-06-17T09:03"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Composite key for both formats keys <- paste(dm$USUBJID, dm$SUBJID, sep = "|")

previous_headings: Example 21: Consistent Data-Driven Formats with fmap()
what: Same fmap(keys, values) pattern for both types
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: formats use identical calling style — fmap(keys, values) keys input lookup values values output objects: extra parameters needed — fmap() tells fnew() use natural direction types.
code: # Date lookup fnew(   fmap(keys, as.Date(dm$RFICDTC, format = "%Y-%m-%d")),   .other      = NA,   type        = "Date",   ignore_case = TRUE,   name        = "icdtn" )  # Character lookup — same fmap(keys, values) pattern! fnew(   fmap(keys, dm$RFICDTC),   .other      = "NOT FOUND",   type        = "character",   ignore_case = TRUE,   name        = "icdtc" )  fprint("icdtn") #> KS Format:icdtn (nocase) #> Type: Date  #> Mappings: #>   SUBJ-001|001 => 2023-03-09 #>   SUBJ-002|002 => 2024-08-13 #>   SUBJ-003|003 => 2025-06-17 fprint("icdtc") #> KS Format:icdtc (nocase) #> Type: character  #> Mappings: #>   SUBJ-001|001 => 2023-03-09T08:45 #>   SUBJ-002|002 => 2024-08-13T09:53 #>   SUBJ-003|003 => 2025-06-17T09:03 #>   .other => NOT FOUND # Both return the expected results fputk("SUBJ-001", "001", format = "icdtn") #> [1] "2023-03-09" class(fputk("SUBJ-001", "001", format = "icdtn")) #> [1] "Date"  fputk("SUBJ-001", "001", format = "icdtc") #> [1] "2023-03-09T08:45" class(fputk("SUBJ-001", "001", format = "icdtc")) #> [1] "character"  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

previous_headings: Example 21: Consistent Data-Driven Formats with fmap()
what: When to use the default (reversal on)
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: default auto-reversal preserves standard R convention c(Label = "Code") maps Code -> Label. natural hand-written formats:
code: # These are equivalent — both map "M" -> "Male" fmt_a <- fnew(c(Male = "M", Female = "F")) fmt_b <- fnew("M" = "Male", "F" = "Female")  identical(fput(c("M", "F"), fmt_a), fput(c("M", "F"), fmt_b)) #> [1] TRUE  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 22: Date Lookup via fparse() and fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Examples 20–21 built composite-key formats programmatically fnew() fmap(). mapping small known advance (e.g., study-specific visit schedule), can define lookup entirely text fparse().

previous_headings: Example 22: Date Lookup via fparse() and fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: simplest approach: store dates character strings using regular character format. Apply fputk() look visit dates questionnaire domain:
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE svdtc (character, nocase)   "SUBJ-001|1" = "2025-01-15"   "SUBJ-001|2" = "2025-02-20"   "SUBJ-001|3" = "2025-03-10"   "SUBJ-002|1" = "2025-01-18"   "SUBJ-002|2" = "2025-02-25"   .other       = "NOT FOUND" ; ')  fprint("svdtc") #> KS Format:svdtc (nocase) #> Type: character  #> Mappings: #>   SUBJ-001|1 => 2025-01-15 #>   SUBJ-001|2 => 2025-02-20 #>   SUBJ-001|3 => 2025-03-10 #>   SUBJ-002|1 => 2025-01-18 #>   SUBJ-002|2 => 2025-02-25 #>   .other => NOT FOUND QS <- data.frame(   USUBJID  = c("SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-001", "SUBJ-002", "SUBJ-002", "SUBJ-002"),   VISITNUM = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 3),   QSSTRESN = c(85, 90, 72, 78, NA),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  QS$SVSTDTC <- fputk(QS$USUBJID, QS$VISITNUM, format = "svdtc") QS #>    USUBJID VISITNUM QSSTRESN    SVSTDTC #> 1 SUBJ-001        1       85 2025-01-15 #> 2 SUBJ-001        2       90 2025-02-20 #> 3 SUBJ-002        1       72 2025-01-18 #> 4 SUBJ-002        2       78 2025-02-25 #> 5 SUBJ-002        3       NA  NOT FOUND  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

previous_headings: Example 22: Date Lookup via fparse() and fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Use Date value type format: store dates native R Date objects. format: parameter tells fparse() parse date strings text block: Now fputk() returns real Date objects — arithmetic comparison work directly:
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE svdtn (Date, format: %Y-%m-%d, nocase)   "SUBJ-001|1" = "2025-01-15"   "SUBJ-001|2" = "2025-02-20"   "SUBJ-001|3" = "2025-03-10"   "SUBJ-002|1" = "2025-01-18"   "SUBJ-002|2" = "2025-02-25" ; ')  fprint("svdtn") #> KS Format:svdtn (nocase) #> Type: Date  #> Mappings: #>   SUBJ-001|1 => 2025-01-15 #>   SUBJ-001|2 => 2025-02-20 #>   SUBJ-001|3 => 2025-03-10 #>   SUBJ-002|1 => 2025-01-18 #>   SUBJ-002|2 => 2025-02-25 QS$SVSTDTC_DT <- fputk(QS$USUBJID, QS$VISITNUM, format = "svdtn") QS #>    USUBJID VISITNUM QSSTRESN    SVSTDTC SVSTDTC_DT #> 1 SUBJ-001        1       85 2025-01-15 2025-01-15 #> 2 SUBJ-001        2       90 2025-02-20 2025-02-20 #> 3 SUBJ-002        1       72 2025-01-18 2025-01-18 #> 4 SUBJ-002        2       78 2025-02-25 2025-02-25 #> 5 SUBJ-002        3       NA  NOT FOUND       <NA>  class(QS$SVSTDTC_DT)         # Date #> [1] "Date" is.na(QS$SVSTDTC_DT[5])      # TRUE — no match for SUBJ-002 Visit 3 #> [1] TRUE  # Date arithmetic works directly QS$SVSTDTC_DT + 7 #> [1] "2025-01-22" "2025-02-27" "2025-01-25" "2025-03-04" NA

previous_headings: Example 22: Date Lookup via fparse() and fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Formats created fparse() can exported back text fexport() re-parsed — useful version-controlled format definitions:
code: fmt_obj <- format_get("svdtn") txt <- fexport(svdtn = fmt_obj) cat(txt) #> VALUE svdtn (Date, nocase, format: %Y-%m-%d) #>   "SUBJ-001|1" = "2025-01-15" #>   "SUBJ-001|2" = "2025-02-20" #>   "SUBJ-001|3" = "2025-03-10" #>   "SUBJ-002|1" = "2025-01-18" #>   "SUBJ-002|2" = "2025-02-25" #> ; # Re-parse the exported text fclear() #> All formats cleared from library. fparse(text = txt)  # Verify it still works fputk("SUBJ-001", 2, format = "svdtn") #> [1] "2025-02-20"  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 23: Inspecting Range Rules with franges()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: franges() extracts range-based mappings format returns tidy data.frame — useful auditing, documentation, downstream processing. can use result like data frame — filter, display, feed calculations: franges() silently excludes discrete entries (.missing, ., plain string keys) — range rows appear. returns empty data.frame columns format contains ranges.
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE age (numeric)   [0, 18)    = "Child"   [18, 65)   = "Adult"   [65, HIGH] = "Senior"   .missing   = "Unknown" ; ')  franges("age") #>   low high inc_low inc_high  label #> 1   0   18    TRUE    FALSE  Child #> 2  18   65    TRUE    FALSE  Adult #> 3  65  Inf    TRUE     TRUE Senior df <- franges("age")  # Which ranges have a finite upper bound? df[is.finite(df$high), ] #>   low high inc_low inc_high label #> 1   0   18    TRUE    FALSE Child #> 2  18   65    TRUE    FALSE Adult fnew("M" = "Male", "F" = "Female", .missing = "Unknown", name = "sex") franges("sex")   # 0 rows #> [1] low      high     inc_low  inc_high label    #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)

previous_headings: Example 23: Inspecting Range Rules with franges()
what: Completing a Summary Against Format Labels
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: can also use labels returned franges() full category set tidyr::complete(), without converting column factor first.
code: age_levels <- franges("age")$label  ages <- c(5, 17, 18, 42)  dplyr::tibble(   age_group = fputn(ages, "age") ) |>   dplyr::count(age_group, name = "n") |>   tidyr::complete(age_group = age_levels, fill = list(n = 0)) #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   age_group     n #>   <chr>     <int> #> 1 Adult         2 #> 2 Child         2 #> 3 Senior        0

previous_headings: Example 23: Inspecting Range Rules with franges()
what: Getting Unique Labels from a Format
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: format already defined, can inspect label values directly stored mappings. aesev example, labels plain character values, unique() gives full set without needing factor().
code: fnew(   fmap(     1:5,     c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe", "Life-threatening", "Death")   ),   type = "numeric",   name = "aesev" )  aesev_labels <- unique(unlist(format_get("aesev")$mappings, use.names = FALSE)) aesev_labels #> [1] "Mild"             "Moderate"         "Severe"           "Life-threatening" #> [5] "Death"

what: Example 24: Reverse Range Lookup with fmap_to_ranges()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: range format stores numeric codes labels (e.g. visit windows coded weeks), fmap_to_ranges() turns vector codes back original [low, high] bounds — one row per input value. Unmatched values produce NA rows, making safe pass arbitrary vectors:
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE visit_ther (numeric)   [LOW,  1] =  0   [ 8, 22] =  2   [22, 36] =  4   [37, 50] =  6   [51, 63] =  8   [64, 78] = 10   [79, 91] = 12 ; ')  coded_weeks <- c(0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12) fmap_to_ranges(coded_weeks, "visit_ther") #>    low high inc_low inc_high #> 1 -Inf    1    TRUE     TRUE #> 2    8   22    TRUE     TRUE #> 3   22   36    TRUE     TRUE #> 4   37   50    TRUE     TRUE #> 5   51   63    TRUE     TRUE #> 6   64   78    TRUE     TRUE #> 7   79   91    TRUE     TRUE fmap_to_ranges(c(2, 99, 4), "visit_ther") #>   low high inc_low inc_high #> 1   8   22    TRUE     TRUE #> 2  NA   NA      NA       NA #> 3  22   36    TRUE     TRUE

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: date_range datetime_range formats bucket Date POSIXct input character labels using ISO date/datetime interval bounds. reuse range-table engine numeric ranges, findInterval() fast path active sorted, disjoint buckets.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: LOW HIGH represent −∞-\infty +∞+\infty — date cutpoint falls open arm.

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Formats export ISO date bounds re-parse without loss:
code: q_obj <- format_get("quarter") cat(fexport(quarter = q_obj)) #> VALUE quarter (date_range) #>   [2024-01-01, 2024-04-01) = "Q1-2024" #>   [2024-04-01, 2024-07-01) = "Q2-2024" #>   [2024-07-01, 2024-10-01) = "Q3-2024" #>   [2024-10-01, 2025-01-01) = "Q4-2024" #>   .other = "Outside 2024" #> ; # Re-parse the exported text txt <- fexport(quarter = q_obj) fclear() #> All formats cleared from library. fparse(text = txt)  fput(as.Date(c("2024-02-14", "2024-08-08")), "quarter") #> [1] "Q1-2024" "Q3-2024"

what: Overlapping buckets with multilabel and fput_all()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE study_window (date_range, multilabel)   [2024-01-01, 2024-07-01) = "First Half"   [2024-04-01, 2024-10-01) = "Mid-Year"   [2024-07-01, 2025-01-01) = "Second Half" ; ')  checkup_dates <- as.Date(c("2024-02-15", "2024-05-20", "2024-09-01")) all_windows   <- fput_all(checkup_dates, "study_window")  for (i in seq_along(checkup_dates)) {   cat(format(checkup_dates[i]), "->",       paste(all_windows[[i]], collapse = " | "), "\n") } #> 2024-02-15 -> First Half  #> 2024-05-20 -> First Half | Mid-Year  #> 2024-09-01 -> Mid-Year | Second Half

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: explicit type given, fparse() infers date_range ISO date bounds datetime_range bounds include time component:
code: fparse(text = ' VALUE auto_fy   [2024-01-01, 2025-01-01) = "2024" ;  VALUE auto_shift   [2024-01-15 08:00, 2024-01-15 16:00) = "Day shift" ; ')  cat("auto_fy type   :", format_get("auto_fy")$type, "\n") #> auto_fy type   : date_range cat("auto_shift type:", format_get("auto_shift")$type, "\n") #> auto_shift type: datetime_range

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: datetime_range works identically date_range matches POSIXct values. Bounds expressed YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM[:SS] strings.

what: Example 26: Stratified Range Lookup with fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: stratified_range type combines discrete stratum (study arm, subject id, composite key) numeric / Date / POSIXct range. stratum bucket boundaries, fputk() dispatches right bucket row.

previous_headings: Example 26: Stratified Range Lookup with fputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: visits <- fmap_strata(   stratum = c("ARM_A", "ARM_A", "ARM_A", "ARM_B", "ARM_B"),   low     = c(0,        7,       28,      0,       14),   high    = c(7,        28,      Inf,     14,      Inf),   label   = c("Baseline", "Wk1-3", "Wk4+", "Baseline", "Wk2+"),   inc_high = c(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE) ) fnew(visits, type = "stratified_range",      ".other|ARM_A" = "A_outside",      .other = "outside_window",      name = "vw")  df <- data.frame(   arm = c("ARM_A", "ARM_A", "ARM_B", "ARM_B", "ARM_C"),   day = c(3,        35,      5,       40,      10) ) df$visit <- fputk(df$arm, df$day, format = "vw") df #>     arm day          visit #> 1 ARM_A   3       Baseline #> 2 ARM_A  35           Wk4+ #> 3 ARM_B   5       Baseline #> 4 ARM_B  40           Wk2+ #> 5 ARM_C  10 outside_window

what: Example 27: Plain Range Lookup with fmap_ranges()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: non-stratified numeric / Date ranges, fmap_ranges() saves hand-crafting canonical keys.
code: age_groups <- fmap_ranges(   low   = c(0, 18, 65),   high  = c(18, 65, Inf),   label = c("Child", "Adult", "Senior"),   inc_high = c(FALSE, FALSE, TRUE) ) fnew(age_groups, type = "numeric", name = "ag") fput(c(5, 25, 90), "ag") #> [1] "Child"  "Adult"  "Senior" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 28: Composite Key Lookup with NA Components (na_as_string)
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: building format data using fmap(paste(..., sep = "|"), values), base R’s paste() converts NA component literal string "NA". resulting composite key therefore "CAT|TEST|NA", missing value. default, fputk() restores NA_character_ wherever component NA lookup — key "CAT|TEST|NA" never reached row falls ./ .missing. Setting na_as_string = TRUE keeps paste()’s literal "NA", making round-trip consistent.

previous_headings: Example 28: Composite Key Lookup with NA Components (na_as_string)
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: common ADaM task: derive PARAMCD combination LBCAT, LBSPEC, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESU, rows LBSTRESU = NA (dimensionless tests INR). Build format fmap(paste(...), PARAMCD).paste() converts NA LBSTRESU "NA", stored keys INR rows "COAGULATION PANEL|BLOOD|INR|NA" "COAGULOGRAM|BLOOD|INR|NA". Now apply format. default na_as_string = FALSE, INR rows get NA (match): na_as_string = TRUE, paste() also converts lookup-side NA "NA", keys match:
code: # Source lab mapping (as received from a specification) lb_map <- data.frame(   LBCAT    = c("BLOOD CHEMISTRY", "COAGULOGRAM", "COAGULATION PANEL", "COAGULOGRAM"),   LBSPEC   = c("BLOOD",           "BLOOD",        "BLOOD",             "BLOOD"),   LBTESTCD = c("ALB",             "FIBRINO",      "INR",               "INR"),   LBSTRESU = c("g/L",             "g/L",           NA,                  NA),   PARAMCD  = c("ALB",             "FIBRINO",       "INR",               "INR"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) lb_map #>               LBCAT LBSPEC LBTESTCD LBSTRESU PARAMCD #> 1   BLOOD CHEMISTRY  BLOOD      ALB      g/L     ALB #> 2       COAGULOGRAM  BLOOD  FIBRINO      g/L FIBRINO #> 3 COAGULATION PANEL  BLOOD      INR     <NA>     INR #> 4       COAGULOGRAM  BLOOD      INR     <NA>     INR with(lb_map,   fmap(paste(LBCAT, LBSPEC, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESU, sep = "|"), PARAMCD) ) |>   fnew(ignore_case = TRUE, .other = NA,        type = "character", name = "lb_param")  fprint("lb_param") #> KS Format:lb_param (nocase) #> Type: character  #> Mappings: #>   BLOOD CHEMISTRY|BLOOD|ALB|g/L => ALB #>   COAGULOGRAM|BLOOD|FIBRINO|g/L => FIBRINO #>   COAGULATION PANEL|BLOOD|INR|NA => INR #>   COAGULOGRAM|BLOOD|INR|NA => INR #>   .other => NA lb_map$PARAMCD_default <- with(lb_map,   fputk(LBCAT, LBSPEC, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESU, format = "lb_param") ) lb_map[, c("LBTESTCD", "LBSTRESU", "PARAMCD", "PARAMCD_default")] #>   LBTESTCD LBSTRESU PARAMCD PARAMCD_default #> 1      ALB      g/L     ALB             ALB #> 2  FIBRINO      g/L FIBRINO         FIBRINO #> 3      INR     <NA>     INR            <NA> #> 4      INR     <NA>     INR            <NA> lb_map$PARAMCD_back <- with(lb_map,   fputk(LBCAT, LBSPEC, LBTESTCD, LBSTRESU,         format = "lb_param", na_as_string = TRUE) ) lb_map[, c("LBTESTCD", "LBSTRESU", "PARAMCD", "PARAMCD_back")] #>   LBTESTCD LBSTRESU PARAMCD PARAMCD_back #> 1      ALB      g/L     ALB          ALB #> 2  FIBRINO      g/L FIBRINO      FIBRINO #> 3      INR     <NA>     INR          INR #> 4      INR     <NA>     INR          INR

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Use na_as_string = TRUE whenever format built withfmap(paste(...), values) key column can contain NA. format keys set hand (fnew("CAT|TEST|g/L" = "ALB", ...)), NA components still go .missing — keep default na_as_string = FALSE.

what: Example 29: Composite Label Invalue Lookup with finputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: finputk() invalue-side mirror fputk(): pastes multiple columns composite label reverse-looks ks_invalue format. na_as_string argument applies.

previous_headings: Example 29: Composite Label Invalue Lookup with finputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
code: # Build an INVALUE from two-column composite labels finput(   fmap(paste(c("BLOOD CHEMISTRY", "COAGULOGRAM", "COAGULATION PANEL"),              c("ALB",             "FIBRINO",      "INR"),              sep = "|"),        c(1L, 2L, 3L)),   target_type = "integer",   name = "lb_code_inv" ) #> KS Invalue: lb_code_inv #> Target Type: integer  #> Mappings: #>   BLOOD CHEMISTRY|ALB => 1 #>   COAGULOGRAM|FIBRINO => 2 #>   COAGULATION PANEL|INR => 3  # Reverse lookup: two separate columns → integer code cat_vec  <- c("BLOOD CHEMISTRY", "COAGULOGRAM", "COAGULATION PANEL", "OTHER") test_vec <- c("ALB",              "FIBRINO",     "INR",               "X")  finputk(cat_vec, test_vec, invalue_name = "lb_code_inv") #> [1]  1  2  3 NA # BLOOD CHEMISTRY|ALB → 1, COAGULOGRAM|FIBRINO → 2, # COAGULATION PANEL|INR → 3, OTHER|X → NA (no match → missing_value) fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

previous_headings: Example 29: Composite Label Invalue Lookup with finputk()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: INVALUE built data containing NA columns, use na_as_string = TRUE build side (fmap(paste(...), ...)) lookup side (finputk(..., na_as_string = TRUE)). output type always determined stored invalue’s target_type (integer). character output create invalue target_type = "character" finputk() returns character vector.
code: # INVALUE where LBSTRESU can be NA (like INR) finput(   fmap(     paste(lb_map$LBCAT, lb_map$LBTESTCD, lb_map$LBSTRESU, sep = "|"),     seq_len(nrow(lb_map))   ),   target_type = "integer",   name = "lb_row_inv" ) #> KS Invalue: lb_row_inv #> Target Type: integer  #> Mappings: #>   BLOOD CHEMISTRY|ALB|g/L => 1 #>   COAGULOGRAM|FIBRINO|g/L => 2 #>   COAGULATION PANEL|INR|NA => 3 #>   COAGULOGRAM|INR|NA => 4  # Reconstruct lb_map row indices — works even when LBSTRESU is NA finputk(lb_map$LBCAT, lb_map$LBTESTCD, lb_map$LBSTRESU,         invalue_name = "lb_row_inv", na_as_string = TRUE) #> [1] 1 2 3 4  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Example 30: Extract Discrete Levels with flevels()
title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: flevels() returns tidy two-column lookup (value, label) ks_format object (registered format name). handy want inspect, join, export exact mapping table used format. result regular data.frame, can filter/select preferred tools:
code: fnew(   "PBO"  = "Placebo",   "D50"  = "Drug A 50 mg",   "D100" = "Drug A 100 mg",   name = "trt" )  flevels("trt") #>   value         label #> 1   PBO       Placebo #> 2   D50  Drug A 50 mg #> 3  D100 Drug A 100 mg lvls <- flevels("trt") lvls[lvls$value != "PBO", , drop = FALSE] #>   value         label #> 2   D50  Drug A 50 mg #> 3  D100 Drug A 100 mg

title: ksformat Usage Examples
text: Numeric pattern formats useful continuous values currency, percentages, fixed-precision displays. Define currency format grouping two decimals: Percent-like suffixes supported via literal text around numeric token:
code: fnew(   "$%,.2f",   .missing = "NO DATA",   .other = "INVALID",   type = "numeric",   name = "currency" )  amounts <- c(1234.56, -7890.12, 0, NA, "oops")  data.frame(   raw = amounts,   formatted = fput(amounts, "currency") ) #>        raw  formatted #> 1  1234.56  $1,234.56 #> 2 -7890.12 -$7,890.12 #> 3        0      $0.00 #> 4     <NA>    NO DATA #> 5     oops    INVALID fnew("%.1f%%", type = "numeric", name = "pct")  values <- c(0, 12.345, -7.8) data.frame(   raw = values,   pct = fputn(values, "pct") ) #>      raw   pct #> 1  0.000  0.0% #> 2 12.345 12.3% #> 3 -7.800 -7.8%

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
text: Format creation — Value--label mappings like SAS PROC FORMAT Format application — Apply formats vectors data frames Reverse formatting — Convert labels back values (INVALUE) Missing value handling — NA, NULL, NaN, empty values Range support — Numeric ranges inclusive/exclusive bounds Multilabel — single value can match multiple labels (fput_all) Expression labels — Dynamic labels .x1, .x2, … evaluated apply-time Case-insensitive matching — ignore_case = TRUE fnew Numeric patterns — %f-style continuous formatting grouping, prefix/suffix text, currency-style output Date/time/datetime — Built-SAS format names custom strftime patterns Import/export — Parse SAS-like text (fparse), export (fexport), import CNTLOUT CSV (fimport) Format library — Register retrieve formats globally

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
code: library(ksformat)  fnew(   "M" = "Male",   "F" = "Female",   .missing = "Unknown",   name = "sex" )  fput(c("M", "F", NA, "X"), "sex") # [1] "Male"    "Female"  "Unknown" "X"

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
text: multilabel = TRUE, single value can match multiple labels. Use fput_all() collect matches:
code: fnew(   "0,17,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Pediatric",   "18,Inf,TRUE,TRUE" = "Adult",   "3,5,TRUE,TRUE"   = "Serious",   name = "ae_age", type = "numeric", multilabel = TRUE )  fput_all(c(10, 25, 4), "ae_age") # [[1]] "Pediatric" # [[2]] "Adult" # [[3]] "Pediatric" "Serious"

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
text: SAS date format names auto-resolved — pre-creation needed: Time (seconds since midnight) datetime also supported:
code: fputn(Sys.Date(), "DATE9.") # [1] "25MAR2026"  fputn(Sys.Date(), "MMDDYY10.") # [1] "03/25/2026"  # Custom strftime pattern fnew_date("%d.%m.%Y", name = "ru_date", type = "date") fput(Sys.Date(), "ru_date") # [1] "25.03.2026" fputn(3600, "TIME8.") # [1] "1:00:00"  fputn(Sys.time(), "DATETIME20.")

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
text: Labels containing .x1, .x2, etc. evaluated R expressions apply-time. Pass extra arguments fput(x, fmt, ...): Use e() mark label evaluation even without .xN placeholders:
code: stat_fmt <- fnew(   "n"   = "sprintf('%s', .x1)",   "pct" = "sprintf('%.1f%%', .x1 * 100)",   name = "stat", type = "character" )  fput(c("n", "pct"), stat_fmt, c(42, 0.053)) # [1] "42"   "5.3%" fnew("ts" = e("format(Sys.time(), '%Y-%m-%d')"), name = "demo") fput("ts", "demo")

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
code: fnew("M" = "Male", "F" = "Female", name = "sex_nc",      type = "character", ignore_case = TRUE)  fput(c("m", "F", "M", "f"), "sex_nc") # [1] "Male"   "Female" "Male"   "Female"

title: SAS-Style PROC FORMAT for R
text: Priority order: NA, NULL, NaN → .missing label defined, otherwise NA Exact match → value–label mapping Range match → range label (numeric formats) match → .label original value Options: keep_na = TRUE, na_if, include_empty = TRUE.

code: # Export a character format sex_fmt <- fnew("M" = "Male", "F" = "Female",                 .missing = "Unknown", name = "sex") cat(fexport(sex = sex_fmt)) #> VALUE sex (character) #>   "M" = "Male" #>   "F" = "Female" #>   .missing = "Unknown" #> ;  # Export a numeric range format fparse(text = ' VALUE bmi (numeric)   [0, 18.5)  = "Underweight"   [18.5, 25) = "Normal"   [25, 30)   = "Overweight"   [30, HIGH] = "Obese"   .missing   = "No data" ; ') bmi_fmt <- format_get("bmi") cat(fexport(bmi = bmi_fmt)) #> VALUE bmi (numeric) #>   [0, 18.5) = "Underweight" #>   [18.5, 25) = "Normal" #>   [25, 30) = "Overweight" #>   [30, HIGH] = "Obese" #>   .missing = "No data" #> ;  # Export a multilabel format risk_fmt <- fnew(   "0,3,TRUE,TRUE" = "Low Risk",   "0,7,TRUE,TRUE" = "Monitored",   "3,7,FALSE,TRUE" = "Medium Risk",   "7,10,FALSE,TRUE" = "High Risk",   name = "risk", type = "numeric", multilabel = TRUE ) cat(fexport(risk = risk_fmt)) #> VALUE risk (numeric, multilabel) #>   [0, 3] = "Low Risk" #>   [0, 7] = "Monitored" #>   (3, 7] = "Medium Risk" #>   (7, 10] = "High Risk" #> ;  # Export a date format enrl_fmt <- fnew_date("DATE9.", name = "enrldt", .missing = "Not Enrolled") cat(fexport(enrldt = enrl_fmt)) #> VALUE enrldt (date) #>   pattern = "DATE9." #>   .missing = "Not Enrolled" #> ; fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

text: 'SAS' format catalogue CSV expected contain standard CNTLOUT columns: FMTNAME, START, END, LABEL, TYPE, HLO, SEXCL, EEXCL. Supported SAS format types: N Numeric VALUE format \(\\) ks_format     type = "numeric" C Character VALUE format \(\\) ks_format     type = "character" Numeric INVALUE (informat) \(\\) ks_invalue     target_type = "numeric" J Character INVALUE (informat) \(\\) ks_invalue     target_type = "character" Incompatible types (logged warning): P PICTURE formats \(-\) equivalent ksformat Rows SAS special missing values (.\(-\).Z, ._) HLO field logged incompatible entries skipped R equivalent concept.

title: Import Formats from 'SAS' PROC FORMAT CNTLOUT CSV — fimport
code: # In SAS: # proc format library=work cntlout=fmts; run; # proc export data=fmts outfile="formats.csv" dbms=csv replace; run;  csv_file <- system.file("extdata", "test_cntlout.csv", package = "ksformat") imported <- fimport(csv_file) #> Warning: Skipping PICTURE format: "PICFMT" #> ℹ TYPE="P" is not supported by ksformat. #> Warning: Skipped incompatible entry in format "SMISSING": #> ✖ SAS special missing value '.A' (HLO='S') has no R equivalent. #> Warning: Skipped incompatible entry in format "SMISSING": #> ✖ SAS special missing value '.B' (HLO='S') has no R equivalent. #> ✔ Imported 4 formats and 1 invalue from #>   /private/var/folders/rn/3s0h46m118j426j_fmjr1z8m0000gn/T/RtmpLTJGZ4/temp_libpathf0d97beed76a/ksformat/extdata/test_cntlout.csv. flist() #> [1] "AGEGRP"   "BMICAT"   "GENDER"   "RACEIN"   "SMISSING" fprint() #> Registered formats: #>   AGEGRP - VALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   BMICAT - VALUE (numeric), 4 mapping(s) #>   GENDER - VALUE (character), 2 mapping(s) #>   RACEIN - INVALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   SMISSING - VALUE (numeric), 1 mapping(s) fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
title: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
text: Creates format object maps values labels, similar 'SAS' PROC FORMAT. Supports discrete value mapping, ranges, special handling missing values. format automatically stored global format library name provided.

title: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
code: fnew(   ...,   name = NULL,   type = "auto",   default = NULL,   multilabel = FALSE,   ignore_case = FALSE,   date_format = NULL,   range_subtype = c("numeric", "date", "datetime"),   strata_sep = "|",   verbose = FALSE )

title: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
text: ... Named arguments defining value-label mappings, one   named vectors/lists using R convention c(Label = "Code").   Can : Discrete values: "M" = "Male", "F" = "Female" Named vector: c(Male = "M", Female = "F") Named list: list(Male = "M", Female = "F") Numeric pattern string: one unnamed %f-style pattern       "%,.2f", "$%,.2f",       "%.1f%%" (type = "numeric") fmap vector: fmap(keys, values)       data-driven formats (reversal) Special values: .missing = "Missing", .= "" Named vectors use R idiom names labels values codes,   reverse ... argument convention. Named-vector reversal: character numeric formats, named   vectors automatically reversed c(Male = "M") becomes   mapping "M" -> "Male" (following factor() convention).   value types ("Date", "POSIXct", "logical"),   reversal occurs — named vector used -names input keys   values output objects, non-character objects serve   vector names. Data-driven formats: formats built programmatically   data, wrap data fmap(keys, values) suppress   automatic reversal types. See   vignette("usage_examples") Example 21 detailed walkthrough. name Character. Optional name format. provided, format automatically registered global format library. type Character. Type format: "character", "numeric", "Date", "POSIXct", "logical", "date_range", "datetime_range", "auto" (default) auto-detection. Value types ("Date", "POSIXct", "logical") store native R objects instead character labels. value types, .missing .always typed NA. Range-bucketing types ("date_range", "datetime_range") bucket Date/POSIXct input character labels using ISO date/datetime strings range bounds. type = "numeric", single unnamed %f-style pattern ... creates numeric pattern format (explicit value-label mappings required). default Character. Default label unmatched values (overrides .) multilabel Logical. TRUE, format supports overlapping ranges single value can match multiple labels. Used fput_all retrieve matching labels. Default FALSE. ignore_case Logical. TRUE, key matching character formats case-insensitive. Default FALSE. date_format Character. Optional strptime-style format string used parsing date/datetime range keys (e.g. "%d/%m/%Y"). NULL, ISO 8601 parsing used. Applies "date_range", "datetime_range", value types "Date" / "POSIXct" keys ranges. range_subtype Character. type = "stratified_range" . One "numeric" (default), "date", "datetime". Determines range part stratified key parsed compared. strata_sep Character. type = "stratified_range" . Single-character (multi-character) separator stratum identifier range key mapping key. Default "|". verbose Logical. TRUE, returns format object visibly; otherwise returns invisibly. Default FALSE.

title: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
text: object class "ks_format" containing format definition.   object also stored format library name given.

title: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
text: Special directives: .missing: Label NA, NULL, NaN values .: Label values matching rule Named-vector direction (reverse convention): named vector list passed unnamed argument (e.g., fnew(c(Male = "M"))), direction name--value mapping depends output type: character / numeric types, names labels     values codes. pairs reversed internally     format maps code -> label. follows standard R idiom used     factor(), c(Label = "Code"). value types (Date, POSIXct,     logical), names input keys values native R     objects returned format. reversal applied,     non-character objects used vector names. means data may need arranged differently depending target type. avoid inconsistency data-driven formats, use fmap(keys, values) works identically types: doubt, use explicit key = "label" arguments — never reversed regardless type. Expression labels: label contains .x1, .x2, etc., treated R expression evaluated apply-time. Extra arguments passed positionally via ... fput: Numeric pattern mode: type = "numeric", may pass one unnamed %f-style pattern string ... instead mapping keys. creates continuous numeric display format: pattern may include literal prefix/suffix text exactly one numeric conversion token (%f / %F flags/width/precision).
code: fnew(fmap(ids, dates), type = "Date") fnew(fmap(ids, date_strings), type = "character") stat_fmt <- fnew("n" = "sprintf('%s', .x1)",                  "pct" = "sprintf('%.1f%%', .x1 * 100)") fput(c("n", "pct"), stat_fmt, c(42, 0.15)) # Returns: "42" "15.0%" fnew("$%,.2f", name = "currency", type = "numeric") fnew("%.1f%%", name = "pct", type = "numeric")

title: Create a Format Definition (like 'SAS' PROC FORMAT) — fnew
code: # Discrete value format (auto-stored as "sex") fnew(   "M" = "Male",   "F" = "Female",   .missing = "Unknown",   .other = "Other Gender",   name = "sex" )  # Apply immediately fput(c("M", "F", NA, "X"), "sex") #> [1] "Male"         "Female"       "Unknown"      "Other Gender" # [1] "Male" "Female" "Unknown" "Other Gender" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # Multilabel format: a value can match multiple labels fnew(   "0,5,TRUE,TRUE"   = "Infant",   "6,11,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Child",   "12,17,TRUE,TRUE" = "Adolescent",   "0,17,TRUE,TRUE"  = "Pediatric",   "18,64,TRUE,TRUE" = "Adult",   "65,Inf,TRUE,TRUE" = "Elderly",   "18,Inf,TRUE,TRUE" = "Non-Pediatric",   name = "age_categories",   type = "numeric",   multilabel = TRUE )  # fput returns first match; fput_all returns all matches fput(c(3, 14, 25, 70), "age_categories") #> [1] "Infant"        "Pediatric"     "Adult"         "Non-Pediatric" fput_all(c(3, 14, 25, 70), "age_categories") #> [[1]] #> [1] "Infant"    "Pediatric" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "Pediatric"  "Adolescent" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "Adult"         "Non-Pediatric" #>  #> [[4]] #> [1] "Non-Pediatric" "Elderly"       #>  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # From a named vector (Label = Code convention) sex_vec <- fnew(c(Male = "M", Female = "F"), .missing = "Unknown",                name = "sex_vec") fput(c("M", "F", NA), sex_vec) #> [1] "Male"    "Female"  "Unknown" # [1] "Male" "Female" "Unknown" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # Numeric pattern format fnew("$%,.2f", name = "currency", type = "numeric") fputn(c(1234.56, -7890.12, 0), "currency") #> [1] "$1,234.56"  "-$7,890.12" "$0.00"      # [1] "$1,234.56" "-$7,890.12" "$0.00" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

title: Create Date/Time Format — fnew_date
code: fnew_date(pattern, name = NULL, type = "auto", .missing = NULL)

title: Create Date/Time Format — fnew_date
text: pattern Character. Either SAS format name (e.g., "DATE9.", "MMDDYY10.", "TIME8.", "DATETIME20.") custom R strftime pattern (e.g., "%Y-%m-%d"). name Character. Name register format . Defaults SAS format name (period) pattern . type Character. Type format: "date", "time", "datetime", "auto" (auto-detect SAS name). Must specified custom strftime patterns. .missing Character. Label missing values (NA). Default NULL.

title: Create Date/Time Format — fnew_date
text: ks_format object date/time type, registered library.

title: Create Date/Time Format — fnew_date
text: SAS format names resolved automatically: Date: DATE9., DDMMYY10., MMDDYY10., YYMMDD10., MONYY7.,         YEAR4., WEEKDATE., WORDDATE., etc. Time: TIME8., TIME5., HHMM., HOUR., MMSS. Datetime: DATETIME20., DATETIME13., etc. Numeric input converted using R epoch ("1970-01-01"): Dates: numeric values interpreted days since 1970-01-01 Datetimes: numeric values interpreted seconds since 1970-01-01 Times: always treated seconds since midnight

title: Create Date/Time Format — fnew_date
code: # Use a SAS format name fnew_date("DATE9.", name = "mydate") #> KS Format:mydate #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %d%b%Y (DATE9.)  fput(as.Date("2020-01-01"), "mydate") #> [1] "01JAN2020" # [1] "01JAN2020"  # Use directly without pre-creating fputn(as.Date("2020-06-15"), "MMDDYY10.") #> [1] "06/15/2020" # [1] "06/15/2020"  # Custom strftime pattern (e.g., Russian style: DD.MM.YYYY) fnew_date("%d.%m.%Y", name = "ru_date", type = "date") #> KS Format:ru_date #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %d.%m.%Y  fput(as.Date(c("1990-03-25", "1985-11-03", "2000-07-14")), "ru_date") #> [1] "25.03.1990" "03.11.1985" "14.07.2000"  # Custom format with missing value label fnew_date("MMDDYY10.", name = "us_date", .missing = "NO DATE") #> KS Format:us_date #> Type: date  #> Pattern: %m/%d/%Y (MMDDYY10.)  #>   .missing => NO DATE fput(c(as.Date("2025-01-01"), NA, as.Date("2025-12-31")), "us_date") #> [1] "01/01/2025" "NO DATE"    "12/31/2025" # [1] "01/01/2025" "NO DATE" "12/31/2025"  # Numeric dates (days since 1970-01-01, R epoch) r_days <- as.numeric(as.Date("2025-01-01")) fputn(r_days, "DATE9.") #> [1] "01JAN2025"  # Multiple SAS date formats applied directly today <- Sys.Date() fputn(today, "DATE9.") #> [1] "09JUL2026" fputn(today, "MMDDYY10.") #> [1] "07/09/2026" fputn(today, "YYMMDD10.") #> [1] "2026-07-09" fputn(today, "MONYY7.") #> [1] "JUL2026" fputn(today, "WORDDATE.") #> [1] "July 09, 2026" fputn(today, "QTR.") #> [1] "3"  # Time formatting (seconds since midnight) fputn(c(0, 3600, 45000, 86399), "TIME8.") #> [1] "0:00:00"  "1:00:00"  "12:30:00" "23:59:59" fputn(c(0, 3600, 45000), "HHMM.") #> [1] "00:00" "01:00" "12:30"  # Datetime formatting now <- Sys.time() fputn(now, "DATETIME20.") #> [1] "09JUL2026:14:51:34" fputn(now, "DTDATE.") #> [1] "09JUL2026" fputn(now, "DTYYMMDD.") #> [1] "2026-07-09" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.

what: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
title: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
text: Reads format definitions written human-friendly 'SAS'-like syntax returns list ks_format /ks_invalue objects. parsed formats automatically stored global format library.

title: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
code: fparse(text = NULL, file = NULL, verbose = FALSE)

title: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
text: text Character string character vector containing format definitions. character vector, lines concatenated newlines. file Path text file containing format definitions. Exactly one text file must provided. verbose Logical. TRUE, parsed formats printed console.  Default FALSE suppress output (result returned invisibly).

title: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
text: named list ks_format /ks_invalue objects.   Names correspond format names defined text.   formats automatically registered global format library.

title: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
text: syntax supports two block types: VALUE blocks define formats (value -> label): INVALUE blocks define reverse formats (label -> numeric value): Syntax rules: Blocks start VALUE INVALUE keyword end ; type parentheses optional; defaults "auto" VALUE,         "numeric" INVALUE   \item Use pattern = "..." date/time/datetime display patterns         pattern: "..." block header numeric display patterns Values can quoted unquoted Ranges use interval notation explicit bounds Legacy range syntax low - high also supported Special range keywords: LOW (-Inf) HIGH (Inf) .missing .special directives Lines starting /*, *, //, # comments Block options: Comma-separated options can placed inside parentheses type: nocase — enables case-insensitive key matching (equivalent         ignore_case = TRUE fnew). multilabel — allows overlapping ranges single value         matches multiple labels (used fput_all). Options can combined: VALUE name (character, nocase, multilabel).
code: VALUE name (type)   "value1" = "Label 1"   "value2" = "Label 2"   [low, high) = "Range Label (half-open)"   (low, high] = "Range Label (open-low, closed-high)"   pattern = "..."  # date/time/datetime patterns, or numeric display patterns   .missing = "Missing Label"   .other = "Other Label" ; INVALUE name   "Label 1" = 1   "Label 2" = 2 ;

title: Parse Format Definitions from 'SAS'-like Text — fparse
code: # Parse multiple format definitions from text fparse(text = ' VALUE sex (character)   "M" = "Male"   "F" = "Female"   .missing = "Unknown" ;  VALUE age (numeric)   [0, 18)    = "Child"   [18, 65)   = "Adult"   [65, HIGH]  = "Senior"   .missing   = "Age Unknown" ;  // Invalue block INVALUE race_inv   "White" = 1   "Black" = 2   "Asian" = 3 ; ')  fput(c("M", "F", NA), "sex") #> [1] "Male"    "Female"  "Unknown" fputn(c(5, 25, 70, NA), "age") #> [1] "Child"       "Adult"       "Senior"      "Age Unknown" finputn(c("White", "Black"), "race_inv") #> [1] 1 2 flist() #> [1] "age"      "race_inv" "sex"      fprint() #> Registered formats: #>   age - VALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   race_inv - INVALUE (numeric), 3 mapping(s) #>   sex - VALUE (character), 2 mapping(s) fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # Parse date/time/datetime format definitions fparse(text = ' VALUE enrldt (date)   pattern = "DATE9."   .missing = "Not Enrolled" ;  VALUE visit_time (time)   pattern = "TIME8." ;  VALUE stamp (datetime)   pattern = "DATETIME20." ; ')  fput(as.Date("2025-03-01"), "enrldt") #> [1] "01MAR2025" fput(36000, "visit_time") #> [1] "10:00:00" fput(as.POSIXct("2025-03-01 10:00:00", tz = "UTC"), "stamp") #> [1] "01MAR2025:10:00:00" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # Numeric display pattern format fparse(text = ' VALUE currency (numeric, pattern: "$%,.2f")   .missing = "NO DATA" ; ') fputn(c(1234.56, -7890.12, NA), "currency") #> [1] "$1,234.56"  "-$7,890.12" "NO DATA"    fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # Case-insensitive format (nocase option) fparse(text = ' VALUE yesno (character, nocase)   "Y" = "Yes"   "N" = "No"   .other = "Unknown" ; ') fput(c("y", "N", "YES"), "yesno") #> [1] "Yes"     "No"      "Unknown" # [1] "Yes" "No" "Unknown" fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.  # Parse multilabel format fparse(text = ' VALUE risk (numeric, multilabel)   [0, 3]  = "Low Risk"   [0, 7]  = "Monitored"   (3, 7]  = "Medium Risk"   (7, 10] = "High Risk" ; ') fput_all(c(2, 5, 9), "risk") #> [[1]] #> [1] "Low Risk"  "Monitored" #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] "Monitored"   "Medium Risk" #>  #> [[3]] #> [1] "High Risk" #>  fclear() #> All formats cleared from library.
